1:
"Rebuke not an elder, but intreat him as a father; and the younger men as brethren;" The witnesses note the charge to rebuke the aged gently, as a father. Gill notes that by "elder" is meant not an elder in office (those are spoken of afterward) but an elder in age, opposed not to a private member but to young men; the apostle is giving rules for rebuking members of the church according to their age. The witnesses note Paul charging that Timothy not sharply rebuke an aged man, but entreat him as a father, and the younger men as brethren; the tender, age-fitting manner of admonition — that Paul directs the spirit in which Timothy is to deal with those he must correct: an aged man is not to be harshly "rebuked" (sharply reproved) but "intreated" (gently exhorted and appealed to) "as a father," with the reverence due to age; and "the younger men" are to be treated "as brethren," with the affection of an equal — so that the minister's correction is always tempered by love and respect suited to each; intreat him as a father; and the younger men as brethren. The witnesses note rebuke not an elder, but intreat him as a father. The witnesses note and the younger men as brethren.

The witnesses note the charge to admonish gently according to age — entreating the aged as a father, the younger men as brethren; correction tempered by love and respect.

So the witnesses make the verse the age-fitting manner of admonition. The lesson the expositors draw is that the minister's correction is to be tempered by reverence to the aged and affection to the young. The witnesses note the elder: "rebuke not an elder" — Gill notes an elder in age, not office; the elder; the in age; the not office; the the aged; the older. The expositors observe the not rebuke: not to harshly rebuke (sharply reprove) the aged; the not rebuke; the not harsh; the not sharp; the the aged; the gentle. The witnesses press the as a father: "but intreat him as a father" — but entreat him as a father, with reverence; the as a father; the entreat; the reverence; the age; the gentle. The expositors draw the lesson on the younger men: "and the younger men as brethren" — the younger men as brethren, with affection; the younger men; the as brethren; the affection; the equal; the love. The witnesses observe the tempered by love: correction is tempered by love and respect; the tempered by love; the respect; the suited; the each; the gentle. The expositors note the rules by age: Paul gives rules for rebuking according to age; the rules by age; the rebuking; the according to age; the the members; the suited. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the rebuke not an elder but intreat him as a father, the and the younger men as brethren, and the age-fitting manner. The verse thus records the age-fitting manner of admonition — teaching that the minister's correction is to be tempered by reverence to the aged and affection to the young.
2:
"The elder women as mothers; the younger as sisters, with all purity." The witnesses note the respectful, pure manner toward women of every age. Gill notes elder women, when they offend, are to be reasoned and pleaded with as children should with their mothers, considered as mothers in Israel, and treated with great tenderness and respect. Barnes notes the same respect for age and the proprieties of life. The witnesses note Paul directing that elder women be treated as mothers, and the younger as sisters, with all purity; the honour and chastity required toward women in the church — that Paul completes the rule of age-fitting respect: "the elder women" are to be regarded "as mothers" (with reverence and tenderness) and "the younger as sisters" (with brotherly affection), and to this he adds a special guard for the latter — "with all purity" — that the minister's dealings with younger women be marked by perfect chastity and freedom from all taint or appearance of impropriety; the younger as sisters, with all purity. The witnesses note the elder women as mothers. The witnesses note the younger as sisters, with all purity.

The witnesses note the respect toward women — elder as mothers, younger as sisters — with the guard of all purity, the minister's dealings free from all impropriety.

So the witnesses make the verse the pure, respectful manner toward women. The lesson the expositors draw is that women are treated with age-fitting honour, the younger with all purity. The witnesses note the elder women: "the elder women as mothers" — Gill notes elder women treated as mothers, with tenderness; the elder women; the as mothers; the reverence; the tenderness; the respect. The expositors observe the as sisters: "the younger as sisters" — the younger women as sisters, with affection; the as sisters; the younger; the affection; the brotherly; the love. The witnesses press the purity: "with all purity" — with all purity (perfect chastity); the purity; the chastity; the perfect; the guard; the clean. The expositors draw the lesson on the no impropriety: the minister's dealings are free from all taint or appearance of impropriety; the no impropriety; the no taint; the no appearance; the clean; the chaste. The witnesses observe the special guard: purity is a special guard for dealings with younger women; the special guard; the younger women; the purity; the the guard; the chaste. The expositors note the age-fitting honour: women are treated with age-fitting honour; the age-fitting honour; the elder; the younger; the respect; the honour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the elder women as mothers, the the younger as sisters with all purity, and the pure respect. The verse thus records the pure, respectful manner toward women — teaching that women are treated with age-fitting honour, the younger with all purity.
3:
"Honour widows that are widows indeed." The witnesses note the duty to honour true widows. Gill notes the honour to be given them is not the putting of them into the office of a deaconess, but rather (as the context shows) the supplying of their needs, maintaining and relieving the truly destitute widows of the church. The witnesses note Paul charging that widows who are widows indeed be honoured; the support and esteem due to the truly destitute widow — that Paul opens his instruction concerning widows by commanding that those "that are widows indeed" — genuinely such, left alone, destitute, and without family to support them — be "honoured," a word that here carries the sense of practical care and maintenance as well as respect, so that the church is to esteem and provide for the widow who has none else to help her; honour widows that are widows indeed. The witnesses note honour widows. The witnesses note that are widows indeed.

The witnesses note the duty to honour true widows — the genuinely destitute, left alone without family — with esteem and practical care and maintenance.

So the witnesses make the verse the support due to true widows. The lesson the expositors draw is that the church is to honour and maintain the widow who is genuinely destitute and alone. The witnesses note the honour: "honour widows" — Gill notes honour carries the sense of support and maintenance; the honour; the support; the maintenance; the care; the esteem. The expositors observe the not deaconess: the honour is not putting them into office, but supplying their need; the not deaconess; the not office; the supply need; the maintain; the relieve. The witnesses press the widows indeed: "that are widows indeed" — genuinely such, left alone and destitute; the widows indeed; the genuinely; the left alone; the destitute; the true. The expositors draw the lesson on the without family: such widows are without family to support them; the without family; the no family; the no support; the alone; the destitute. The witnesses observe the practical care: honour carries practical care and maintenance; the practical care; the maintenance; the provide; the support; the care. The expositors note the esteem: as well as respect and esteem; the esteem; the respect; the honour; the regard; the care. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the honour widows, the that are widows indeed, and the support of true widows. The verse thus records the support due to true widows — teaching that the church is to honour and maintain the widow who is genuinely destitute and alone.
4:
"But if any widow have children or nephews, let them learn first to shew piety at home, and to requite their parents: for that is good and acceptable before God." The witnesses note that families must support their own widows. Gill notes that such widows who have children or nephews (grandchildren) are not widows indeed; they are not desolate or without persons to take care of them, and their relations should provide, and not suffer the church to be burdened with them. The witnesses note Paul directing that if a widow have children or grandchildren, these should learn to show piety at home and requite their parents, for this is good and acceptable before God; the family's first duty to maintain its own — that Paul guards the church's charity by laying the first burden of a widow's care on her own kin: if she has "children or nephews" (descendants), they are to "learn first to shew piety at home" (to practise true godliness in caring for their own household) and "to requite their parents" (repaying the care once given them) — a plain natural and Christian duty that is "good and acceptable before God," so that family love must do its part before the church is asked; let them learn first to shew piety at home, and to requite their parents. The witnesses note let them learn first to shew piety at home. The witnesses note to requite their parents: for that is good and acceptable before God.

The witnesses note the family's first duty — children and grandchildren to show piety at home and requite their parents; family love must do its part before the church.

So the witnesses make the verse the family's first duty to its own widows. The lesson the expositors draw is that a widow's own kin must care for her before the church is burdened, a duty good and acceptable to God. The witnesses note the children: "if any widow have children or nephews" — Gill notes such are not widows indeed, having relations to provide; the children; the nephews; the not widows indeed; the relations; the provide. The expositors observe the piety at home: "let them learn first to shew piety at home" — to practise godliness in caring for their household; the piety at home; the godliness; the the household; the care; the practise. The witnesses press the requite parents: "and to requite their parents" — repaying the care once given them; the requite parents; the repaying; the the care; the given; the duty. The expositors draw the lesson on the natural duty: this is a plain natural and Christian duty; the natural duty; the Christian; the plain; the duty; the family. The witnesses observe the good and acceptable: "for that is good and acceptable before God" — good and acceptable before God; the good and acceptable; the before God; the pleasing; the approved; the good. The expositors note the before the church: family love must do its part before the church is asked; the before the church; the family love; the its part; the the church; the spared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let them learn first to shew piety at home, the to requite their parents for that is good and acceptable before God, and the family's first duty. The verse thus records the family's first duty to its own widows — teaching that a widow's kin must care for her before the church is burdened.
5:
"Now she that is a widow indeed, and desolate, trusteth in God, and continueth in supplications and prayers night and day." The witnesses note the marks of the true widow — desolate, trusting God, given to prayer. Gill notes a real widow is one whom the Jews call "a perfect one," in opposition to a divorced woman or a brother's widow; Barnes notes "desolate" means solitary, alone. The witnesses note Paul describing the widow indeed as one who, being desolate, trusts in God and continues in supplications and prayers night and day; the portrait of the godly, praying widow who casts herself on God — that Paul draws the character of the widow truly deserving the church's care: she is "desolate" (left utterly alone, without husband or family), and in that very destitution she "trusteth in God" (making him her support and hope), and "continueth in supplications and prayers night and day" — a woman of devotion, who, having no earthly stay, gives herself to constant prayer, so that her loneliness is filled with God; trusteth in God, and continueth in supplications and prayers night and day. The witnesses note she that is a widow indeed, and desolate, trusteth in God. The witnesses note continueth in supplications and prayers night and day.

The witnesses note the portrait of the true widow — desolate yet trusting God, continuing in prayer night and day; her loneliness filled with God.

So the witnesses make the verse the portrait of the godly widow. The lesson the expositors draw is that the true widow, left desolate, casts herself on God and gives herself to constant prayer. The witnesses note the desolate: "she that is a widow indeed, and desolate" — Barnes notes desolate means solitary, alone; the desolate; the solitary; the alone; the destitute; the left. The expositors observe the widow indeed: a real widow, "a perfect one," left without husband or family; the widow indeed; the real; the perfect one; the without family; the destitute. The witnesses press the trusteth in God: "trusteth in God" — in her destitution she trusts in God; the trusteth in God; the her support; the her hope; the casts; the trusts. The expositors draw the lesson on the prayer: "continueth in supplications and prayers night and day" — continues in prayer night and day; the prayer; the supplications; the night and day; the constant; the devotion. The witnesses observe the no earthly stay: having no earthly stay, she gives herself to prayer; the no earthly stay; the no stay; the prayer; the devotion; the God. The expositors note the loneliness filled: her loneliness is filled with God; the loneliness filled; the with God; the her destitution; the prayer; the trust. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the she that is a widow indeed and desolate trusteth in God, the continueth in supplications and prayers night and day, and the portrait of the godly widow. The verse thus records the portrait of the godly widow — teaching that the true widow casts herself on God and gives herself to constant prayer.
6:
"But she that liveth in pleasure is dead while she liveth." The witnesses note the contrast — the pleasure-loving widow is spiritually dead. Gill notes she that lives voluptuously and deliciously, a wanton, loose, and licentious life, serving divers lusts and pleasures, is meant. Barnes notes the Greek word means to live luxuriously or in self-indulgence. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the widow who lives in pleasure is dead while she lives; the spiritual deadness of the self-indulgent widow — that Paul sets against the praying widow her opposite: the woman who "liveth in pleasure" (in luxury, self-indulgence, and sensual ease, the slave of her appetites), and pronounces her "dead while she liveth" — though alive in body, she is dead in soul, void of spiritual life and of any title to the church's honour, for a life given to pleasure is a kind of living death, the very reverse of the widow indeed who trusts in God; she that liveth in pleasure is dead while she liveth. The witnesses note she that liveth in pleasure. The witnesses note is dead while she liveth.

The witnesses note the self-indulgent widow "dead while she liveth" — alive in body but dead in soul; a life given to pleasure a kind of living death.

So the witnesses make the verse the spiritual deadness of the self-indulgent. The lesson the expositors draw is that the widow living in pleasure is spiritually dead, the reverse of the praying widow. The witnesses note the liveth in pleasure: "she that liveth in pleasure" — Gill notes living voluptuously, in luxury and self-indulgence; the liveth in pleasure; the voluptuously; the luxury; the self-indulgence; the sensual. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes the word means to live luxuriously, in self-indulgence; the luxuriously; the self-indulgence; the sensual; the ease; the appetites. The witnesses press the dead: "is dead while she liveth" — she is dead while she lives; the dead; the while she liveth; the spiritually dead; the in soul; the living death. The expositors draw the lesson on the dead in soul: though alive in body, she is dead in soul; the dead in soul; the alive in body; the void of life; the spiritual; the dead. The witnesses observe the no title: she has no title to the church's honour; the no title; the the church's honour; the void; the dead; the no claim. The expositors note the living death: a life given to pleasure is a kind of living death; the living death; the pleasure; the death; the reverse; the dead. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the she that liveth in pleasure, the is dead while she liveth, and the spiritual deadness. The verse thus records the spiritual deadness of the self-indulgent — teaching that the widow living in pleasure is spiritually dead, the reverse of the praying widow.
7:
"And these things give in charge, that they may be blameless." The witnesses note the charge that these rules be enforced for blamelessness. Gill notes "these things" concern rebuking persons of different age and sex, the maintenance of widows, that children provide for their parents, and that widows trust in God and give themselves to prayer, not living in sin. Barnes notes to announce or declare them. The witnesses note Paul directing that Timothy give these things in charge, that they may be blameless; the enforcing of the rules so that all be without reproach — that Paul bids Timothy not merely to know these directions but to "give them in charge" (to enjoin them solemnly and publicly upon the people), and that with a clear aim: "that they may be blameless" — that the widows, the families, and all concerned might so order their conduct as to be free from reproach, the rules being given not to burden but to keep the church and its members unblamable; these things give in charge, that they may be blameless. The witnesses note these things give in charge. The witnesses note that they may be blameless.

The witnesses note the charge to enforce these rules — that widows and families be blameless; the rules given not to burden but to keep all without reproach.

So the witnesses make the verse the enforcing of the rules for blamelessness. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is to enjoin these directions so that all be free from reproach. The witnesses note the give in charge: "these things give in charge" — Gill notes Timothy is to enjoin these things solemnly; the give in charge; the enjoin; the solemnly; the publicly; the the people. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes to announce or declare them; the announce; the declare; the enjoin; the charge; the publicly. The witnesses press the these things: "these things" — concerning rebuking, widows, families, prayer; the these things; the rebuking; the widows; the families; the prayer. The expositors draw the lesson on the blameless: "that they may be blameless" — that they may be blameless (free from reproach); the blameless; the free from reproach; the unblamable; the the aim; the clear. The witnesses observe the not to burden: the rules are given not to burden but to keep unblamable; the not to burden; the keep unblamable; the the church; the the members; the order. The expositors note the without reproach: that all be without reproach; the without reproach; the all; the the widows; the families; the blameless. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the these things give in charge, the that they may be blameless, and the enforcing of the rules. The verse thus records the enforcing of the rules for blamelessness — teaching that Timothy is to enjoin these directions so that all be free from reproach.
8:
"But if any provide not for his own, and specially for those of his own house, he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel." The witnesses note the grave guilt of failing to provide for one's family. Chrysostom notes many think their own virtue enough for salvation, but err, as the man who buried his one talent and brought it back entire was still condemned; so it is not enough to keep oneself, one must profit others. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if any provide not for his own, especially his own house, he has denied the faith and is worse than an infidel; the solemn duty of providing for one's family — that Paul presses the family's duty (v.4) with the sharpest warning: the man who "provide not for his own" (his relations), and "specially for those of his own house" (his nearest family, parents, widows, dependents), has by that neglect "denied the faith" — contradicting in deed the love the gospel requires — and "is worse than an infidel," since even unbelievers, by natural affection, provide for their own; so that to fail here is to sink below the very heathen; he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel. The witnesses note if any provide not for his own. The witnesses note he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel.

The witnesses note the grave guilt of failing to provide for one's own — such a man has denied the faith and is worse than an infidel, sinking below the very heathen.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn duty of providing for family. The lesson the expositors draw is that to neglect one's own household is to deny the faith and sink below the unbeliever. The witnesses note the provide not: "if any provide not for his own" — Paul warns the man who provides not for his relations; the provide not; the his own; the his relations; the neglect; the fail. The expositors observe the his own house: "and specially for those of his own house" — especially his nearest family; the his own house; the nearest family; the parents; the dependents; the household. The witnesses press the denied the faith: "he hath denied the faith" — by such neglect he has denied the faith; the denied the faith; the contradicting; the in deed; the the love; the the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the worse than an infidel: "and is worse than an infidel" — worse than an unbeliever; the worse than an infidel; the the unbeliever; the below; the the heathen; the natural affection. The witnesses observe the even heathen provide: even unbelievers, by natural affection, provide for their own; the even heathen; the natural affection; the provide; the their own; the the unbeliever. The expositors note Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes it is not enough to keep oneself, one must profit others; the not enough; the keep oneself; the profit others; the the talent; the condemned. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if any provide not for his own, the he hath denied the faith and is worse than an infidel, and the duty of providing. The verse thus records the solemn duty of providing for family — teaching that to neglect one's own household is to deny the faith and sink below the unbeliever.
9:
"Let not a widow be taken into the number under threescore years old, having been the wife of one man," The witnesses note the qualifications for enrolling a widow. Gill notes "the number" is of widows to be maintained by the church (some take it of those made deaconesses with care of the poor widows, but the former sense is best), and such were not to be taken in under sixty years old. The witnesses note Paul directing that a widow not be taken into the number under sixty years old, having been the wife of one man; the conditions for placing a widow on the church's roll — that Paul, distinguishing which widows are to be formally enrolled for the church's support and service, sets two conditions: she must be "under threescore years old" no longer (that is, at least sixty, past the age of remarriage and of settled character), and "having been the wife of one man" (of approved fidelity in marriage, not given to a loose or many-married life) — so that only widows of mature age and proven chastity are to be received onto the roll; let not a widow be taken into the number under threescore years old. The witnesses note let not a widow be taken into the number under threescore years old. The witnesses note having been the wife of one man.

The witnesses note the conditions for enrolling a widow — at least sixty years old and the wife of one man; only widows of mature age and proven chastity received.

So the witnesses make the verse the conditions for enrolling a widow. The lesson the expositors draw is that only widows of mature age and approved fidelity are to be placed on the church's roll. The witnesses note the number: "let not a widow be taken into the number" — Gill notes the number of widows maintained by the church; the the number; the maintained; the the church; the the roll; the enrolled. The expositors observe the threescore: "under threescore years old" — at least sixty, past remarriage; the threescore; the sixty; the mature age; the past remarriage; the settled. The witnesses press the wife of one man: "having been the wife of one man" — of approved fidelity in marriage; the wife of one man; the fidelity; the approved; the chastity; the not loose. The expositors draw the lesson on the proven chastity: only widows of proven chastity are received; the proven chastity; the approved; the fidelity; the received; the chaste. The witnesses observe the mature age: only widows of mature, settled age; the mature age; the sixty; the settled; the the roll; the received. The expositors note the two conditions: Paul sets two conditions for enrolment; the two conditions; the age; the fidelity; the the roll; the qualifications. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let not a widow be taken into the number under threescore years old, the having been the wife of one man, and the conditions for enrolling. The verse thus records the conditions for enrolling a widow — teaching that only widows of mature age and approved fidelity are placed on the roll.
10:
"Well reported of for good works; if she have brought up children, if she have lodged strangers, if she have washed the saints' feet, if she have relieved the afflicted, if she have diligently followed every good work." The witnesses note the catalogue of good works required of the enrolled widow. Gill notes she must be well reported of for good works, both by members of the church and by them that are without. Barnes notes good character or reputation. The witnesses note Paul listing that the enrolled widow be well reported of for good works — bringing up children, lodging strangers, washing the saints' feet, relieving the afflicted, following every good work; the proven life of holy service that marks the worthy widow — that Paul completes the qualifications with a portrait of practical godliness: the widow must be "well reported of for good works," and he names them — the careful "bringing up of children," the hospitable "lodging of strangers," the humble "washing of the saints' feet" (lowly service to fellow believers), the merciful "relieving of the afflicted," and in sum a diligent following of "every good work" — so that her past life of love and service is the credential for the church's honour; well reported of for good works. The witnesses note well reported of for good works. The witnesses note if she have washed the saints' feet, if she have relieved the afflicted.

The witnesses note the catalogue of good works — bringing up children, lodging strangers, washing the saints' feet, relieving the afflicted; a life of holy service the widow's credential.

So the witnesses make the verse the proven life of service marking the worthy widow. The lesson the expositors draw is that the enrolled widow's credential is a past life of practical godliness and good works. The witnesses note the well reported: "well reported of for good works" — Gill notes well reported by members and those without; the well reported; the good works; the by members; the without; the character. The expositors observe the brought up children: "if she have brought up children" — the careful bringing up of children; the brought up children; the careful; the children; the her own; the others. The witnesses press the lodged strangers: "if she have lodged strangers" — the hospitable lodging of strangers; the lodged strangers; the hospitable; the hospitality; the strangers; the welcomed. The expositors draw the lesson on the washed feet: "if she have washed the saints' feet" — the humble washing of the saints' feet (lowly service); the washed feet; the the saints; the humble; the lowly service; the serve. The witnesses observe the relieved the afflicted: "if she have relieved the afflicted" — the merciful relieving of the afflicted; the relieved the afflicted; the merciful; the the afflicted; the help; the mercy. The expositors note the every good work: "if she have diligently followed every good work" — a diligent following of every good work; the every good work; the diligent; the followed; the good; the service. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the well reported of for good works, the if she have washed the saints' feet if she have relieved the afflicted, and the proven life of service. The verse thus records the proven life of service marking the worthy widow — teaching that her credential is a past life of practical godliness and good works.
11:
"But the younger widows refuse: for when they have begun to wax wanton against Christ, they will marry;" The witnesses note why the younger widows are not to be enrolled. Chrysostom notes that Paul, having settled the age and qualifications of the widow to be admitted, now turns to the younger, who are not to be taken in, because of the risk that their unsubdued desires will draw them back to a worldly life. The witnesses note Paul directing that the younger widows be refused, for when they wax wanton against Christ, they will marry; the reason for not enrolling younger widows — the danger of unsettled desire — that Paul bids Timothy "refuse" (not enrol) the "younger widows," and gives the reason: when such, having pledged themselves to the church's service, "have begun to wax wanton against Christ" (when fleshly desire revives and draws them from their devotion to him), "they will marry" — wishing to return to married life and so breaking the engagement they had made — so that it is unwise to bind the young by such a pledge their changeable hearts may not keep; the younger widows refuse: for when they have begun to wax wanton against Christ. The witnesses note the younger widows refuse. The witnesses note when they have begun to wax wanton against Christ, they will marry.

The witnesses note why younger widows are not enrolled — when fleshly desire revives and draws them from devotion to Christ, they will wish to marry and break their pledge.

So the witnesses make the verse the danger of enrolling younger widows. The lesson the expositors draw is that the young are not to be bound by a pledge their changeable hearts may not keep. The witnesses note the refuse: "the younger widows refuse" — Paul bids refuse (not enrol) the younger widows; the refuse; the not enrol; the younger; the the young; the decline. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes the risk of their unsubdued desires drawing them back; the the risk; the unsubdued desires; the drawing back; the worldly; the danger. The witnesses press the wax wanton: "for when they have begun to wax wanton against Christ" — when fleshly desire revives against their devotion to Christ; the wax wanton; the fleshly desire; the against Christ; the revives; the devotion. The expositors draw the lesson on the will marry: "they will marry" — they will wish to marry, returning to married life; the will marry; the wish to marry; the married life; the return; the break. The witnesses observe the break the pledge: so breaking the engagement they had made; the break the pledge; the the engagement; the made; the broken; the unkept. The expositors note the changeable hearts: the young have changeable hearts that may not keep the pledge; the changeable hearts; the the young; the not keep; the the pledge; the unwise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the younger widows refuse, the when they have begun to wax wanton against Christ they will marry, and the danger of enrolling. The verse thus records the danger of enrolling younger widows — teaching that the young are not to be bound by a pledge their changeable hearts may not keep.
12:
"Having damnation, because they have cast off their first faith." The witnesses note the guilt incurred by breaking the first pledge. Barnes notes it is rather "having condemnation," or incurring guilt — this does not mean of necessity eternal damnation, but the guilt of breaking a solemn engagement. The witnesses note Paul declaring that such have condemnation, because they have cast off their first faith; the guilt of breaking the pledge first given — that Paul names what such younger widows incur by abandoning the service they had vowed: "having damnation" — better "condemnation," that is, blame and guilt — "because they have cast off their first faith," that is, they have broken the first pledge or promise of devotion they had made (to serve Christ and the church in their widowhood), so that the fault lies in the breaking of a solemn engagement, a thing that justly brings reproach; having damnation, because they have cast off their first faith. The witnesses note having damnation. The witnesses note because they have cast off their first faith.

The witnesses note the guilt incurred — "having condemnation," because they have cast off their first faith; the fault lies in breaking a solemn pledge of devotion.

13:
"And withal they learn to be idle, wandering about from house to house; and not only idle, but tattlers also and busybodies, speaking things which they ought not." The witnesses note the idle and meddlesome habits such widows fall into. Gill notes that, being at ease and without labour, living at the expense of the church, they learn to be idle and gad about. Barnes notes that, if supported by the church and without settled principles, other evils are to be feared. The witnesses note Paul observing that such widows learn to be idle, wandering from house to house, and become tattlers and busybodies, speaking what they ought not; the further evils of an idle, gossiping life — that Paul names the additional dangers of enrolling unfit younger widows: maintained without labour, they "learn to be idle" (fall into habits of idleness), "wandering about from house to house" (gadding from home to home with nothing to do), and the idleness breeds worse — they become "tattlers" (gossips) "and busybodies" (meddlers in others' affairs), "speaking things which they ought not" — so that idleness opens the door to a train of mischiefs; tattlers also and busybodies, speaking things which they ought not. The witnesses note they learn to be idle, wandering about from house to house. The witnesses note tattlers also and busybodies, speaking things which they ought not.

14:
"I will therefore that the younger women marry, bear children, guide the house, give none occasion to the adversary to speak reproachfully." The witnesses note Paul's counsel that the younger widows marry and keep house. Gill notes "the younger women" is rather "the younger widows" (so some copies read), for this is not advice to young women in general but to the younger widows of whom Paul speaks. Barnes notes "I give it as my opinion," or counsel. The witnesses note Paul counselling that the younger widows marry, bear children, guide the house, and give the adversary no occasion to reproach; the wholesome remedy of marriage and household duty — that Paul, instead of enrolling the younger widows for idle dependence, gives the better course: let them "marry" again, "bear children," and "guide the house" (manage their households well), filling their lives with the honest duties of wife and mother, and so "give none occasion to the adversary" — whether Satan or the church's enemies — "to speak reproachfully," that their busy, ordered home-life might silence all slander; that the younger women marry, bear children, guide the house. The witnesses note I will therefore that the younger women marry, bear children, guide the house. The witnesses note give none occasion to the adversary to speak reproachfully.

15:
"For some are already turned aside after Satan." The witnesses note that some younger widows had already gone astray. Gill notes Paul means some of those younger widows whom he had observed to depart from the faith they first professed, turn their backs on Christ, and give themselves up to carnal lusts and an idle, impure life, walking after the course of this world and its prince. The witnesses note some are already turned aside. The witnesses note after Satan.

16:
"If any man or woman that believeth have widows, let them relieve them, and let not the church be charged; that it may relieve them that are widows indeed." The witnesses note that believing relatives should relieve their own widows, sparing the church. Gill notes that if any member of a church, brother or sister, has mothers, grandmothers, or near relations who are widows in mean circumstances and unable to care for themselves, they should relieve them. The witnesses note Paul directing that any believing man or woman who has widows relieve them, that the church not be charged, but may relieve the widows indeed; the relief of one's own widows so the church may help the truly destitute — that Paul, closing the matter, again lays the burden of a widow's support on her believing kindred: "if any man or woman that believeth have widows" among their relations, "let them relieve them" privately, "and let not the church be charged" (burdened) with them — to the end "that it may relieve them that are widows indeed," the truly destitute who have no one else — so that family responsibility frees the church's limited resources for those genuinely in need; let not the church be charged; that it may relieve them that are widows indeed. The witnesses note if any man or woman that believeth have widows, let them relieve them. The witnesses note that it may relieve them that are widows indeed.

The witnesses note that believing kindred should relieve their own widows, sparing the church — that its resources be free for the widows indeed, the truly destitute.

17:
"Let the elders that rule well be counted worthy of double honour, especially they who labour in the word and doctrine." The witnesses note the double honour due to faithful elders, especially preachers. Gill notes by "elders" here are meant not elders in age but elders in office, good rulers and labourers in the word; such are to be counted worthy of double honour. The witnesses note Paul directing that elders who rule well be counted worthy of double honour, especially those who labour in the word and doctrine; the honour and maintenance due to faithful, labouring elders — that Paul turns to the church's duty toward its officers: "the elders that rule well" (those who govern the church faithfully and wisely) are to be counted "worthy of double honour" — a double esteem, and (as the next verse shows) double in the sense of including generous maintenance as well as respect — "especially they who labour in the word and doctrine," that is, above all those who toil hard in preaching and teaching, the labour of the word deserving the fullest honour; let the elders that rule well be counted worthy of double honour. The witnesses note let the elders that rule well be counted worthy of double honour. The witnesses note especially they who labour in the word and doctrine.

18:
"For the Scripture saith, Thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn. And, The labourer is worthy of his reward." The witnesses note the Scripture grounds for maintaining the minister. Barnes notes this is adduced as a reason why a church should show all due respect and care for its ministers: as God provided for the labouring ox, much more should due attention be paid to those who labour for the church's welfare. The witnesses note Paul citing the Scripture — that the ox treading out the corn is not to be muzzled, and the labourer is worthy of his reward — to ground the elder's maintenance; the two scriptural warrants for the minister's support — that Paul backs the "double honour" with two proofs: the law's word "Thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn" (which, applied, means the labourer should share in the fruit of his toil), and the saying "The labourer is worthy of his reward" (a principle of natural justice, and the Lord's own word); the witnesses note that Paul here calls both an Old Testament law and a saying of Christ "the Scripture," joining them as of equal authority — so that the minister's right to support rests on God's own word; the labourer is worthy of his reward. The witnesses note thou shalt not muzzle the ox that treadeth out the corn. The witnesses note the labourer is worthy of his reward.

The witnesses note the two scriptural warrants for the minister's support — the unmuzzled ox and "the labourer is worthy of his reward," joined as of equal authority.

So the witnesses make the verse the Scripture grounds for the minister's support. The lesson the expositors draw is that the elder's maintenance rests on God's own word, both law and the saying of Christ. 19:
"Against an elder receive not an accusation, but before two or three witnesses." The witnesses note the safeguard against rash accusations of elders. Barnes notes "elder" here is used as in the previous verse, of office and not of age; the connection demands it. Receiving not an accusation means not regarding such a charge as proved unless duly attested. The witnesses note Paul charging that against an elder no accusation be received except before two or three witnesses; the protection of the minister from unsupported charges — that Paul, having required the elder's honour, guards his good name from malice: "against an elder" (a church officer) Timothy is to "receive not an accusation" — not entertain or act on a charge against him — "but before two or three witnesses," that is, only when it is supported by the testimony of two or three, according to the rule of God's law; so that a faithful minister, peculiarly exposed to slander, is not to be condemned on the word of a single accuser; against an elder receive not an accusation, but before two or three witnesses. The witnesses note against an elder receive not an accusation. The witnesses note but before two or three witnesses.

The witnesses note the safeguard for elders — no accusation received except before two or three witnesses; the faithful minister not condemned on a single accuser's word.

So the witnesses make the verse the protection of the elder from unsupported charges. 20:
"Them that sin rebuke before all, that others also may fear." The witnesses note the open rebuke of those who sin, for the warning of others. Gill notes Paul adds this to show he was far from screening wicked ministers or elders guilty of gross enormities; though it may apply to all offenders who are members, it seems chiefly to regard elders who sin, who continue to sin. The witnesses note Paul charging that those who sin be rebuked before all, that others also may fear; the public rebuke of persistent offenders as a deterrent — that Paul balances the safeguard of v.19 with a charge against partiality: an elder (or any member) duly proved to "sin" — and especially one who persists in open, notorious sin — is to be "rebuke[d] before all" (openly reproved in the presence of the church, not spared because of his office), and that with a clear purpose, "that others also may fear" — that the public reproof might be a salutary warning, deterring others from the like sin; them that sin rebuke before all, that others also may fear. The witnesses note them that sin rebuke before all. The witnesses note that others also may fear.

The witnesses note the open rebuke of persistent offenders before all — not sparing even an elder for his office — that others may fear and be deterred from the like sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the public rebuke of persistent sin. The lesson the expositors draw is that the proven, persistent offender is openly rebuked, that others may fear. The witnesses note the rebuke before all: "them that sin rebuke before all" — Gill notes open rebuke, not screening wicked elders; the rebuke before all; the openly; the not screening; the the church; the reproved. The expositors observe the persistent: this regards chiefly those who continue to sin; the persistent; the continue to sin; the notorious; the open; the gross. The witnesses press the not spared: the offender is not spared because of his office; the not spared; the his office; the the elder; the not screened; the reproved. 21:
"I charge thee before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels, that thou observe these things without preferring one before another, doing nothing by partiality." The witnesses note the solemn charge to impartial judgment. Chrysostom notes that, having spoken of bishops, deacons, men, women, widows, and elders, and shown how great the bishop's authority, Paul now speaks of judgment, charging Timothy before God, Christ, and the elect angels to do nothing by partiality. The witnesses note Paul solemnly charging Timothy, before God, the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels, to observe these things without partiality; the most solemn adjuration to judge without favour or prejudice — that Paul, on the weighty matter of judging elders and offenders, lays the most solemn charge: he adjures Timothy "before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels" (in the awful presence of the whole heavenly court) "that thou observe these things" — the rules just given — "without preferring one before another" (without bias or favouritism) and "doing nothing by partiality" — that in all his judgments Timothy hold the strictest impartiality, as one who acts under the eye of heaven; without preferring one before another, doing nothing by partiality. The witnesses note I charge thee before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the elect angels. The witnesses note without preferring one before another, doing nothing by partiality.

The witnesses note the most solemn charge — before God, Christ, and the elect angels — to observe these things without partiality; Timothy judging under the eye of heaven.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn charge to impartial judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is adjured before the heavenly court to judge without favour or prejudice. 22:
"Lay hands suddenly on no man, neither be partaker of other men's sins: keep thyself pure." The witnesses note the warning against hasty ordination and sharing in others' sins. Gill notes this is not about removing censures, but the laying on of hands in ordination, which is not to be done suddenly or hastily, without due trial and examination of the man's fitness. The witnesses note Paul charging that hands be laid suddenly on no man, that Timothy not partake of others' sins, but keep himself pure; the caution in ordaining and the call to personal purity — that Paul warns against rashness in setting men into office: "lay hands suddenly on no man" (ordain none hastily, without proving his fitness), lest, by ordaining an unworthy man, Timothy become "partaker of other men's sins" (sharing the guilt of the harm such a one does) — and he adds the call to personal holiness, "keep thyself pure," that Timothy guard his own life clean, both from such complicity and from all defilement; lay hands suddenly on no man, neither be partaker of other men's sins: keep thyself pure. The witnesses note lay hands suddenly on no man, neither be partaker of other men's sins. The witnesses note keep thyself pure.

The witnesses note the warning against hasty ordination — lest Timothy share in others' sins by ordaining the unworthy — and the call to keep himself pure.

So the witnesses make the verse the caution in ordaining and the call to purity. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is not to ordain hastily, lest he share others' guilt, but keep himself pure. The expositors observe the due trial: ordination requires due trial and examination of fitness; the due trial; the examination; the fitness; the proving; the the man. The expositors draw the lesson on the unworthy man: ordaining an unworthy man makes one share his sins; the unworthy man; the ordaining; the share; the the sins; the guilt. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the lay hands suddenly on no man neither be partaker of other men's sins, the keep thyself pure, and the caution in ordaining. The verse thus records the caution in ordaining and the call to purity — teaching that Timothy is not to ordain hastily, lest he share others' guilt, but keep himself pure.
23:
"Drink no longer water, but use a little wine for thy stomach's sake and thine often infirmities." The witnesses note the kindly counsel to Timothy concerning his health. Gill notes it was commendable in Timothy to keep under his body by abstemious living, as Paul did, not pampering the flesh; yet it was proper he should care for his health, that it not be impaired by too much severity, lest he be incapable of the Lord's work. The witnesses note Paul advising Timothy to drink no longer only water, but use a little wine for his stomach and his frequent infirmities; the practical, fatherly care for the minister's health — that Paul, with tender concern, breaks in with a word about Timothy's body: his young helper, given to a strict, abstemious life and drinking only water, was suffering from a weak stomach and "often infirmities," and so Paul counsels him to "use a little wine" as a medicine — moderately, "for thy stomach's sake" — so that, while temperance is good, the minister must not by needless austerity ruin the health he needs for God's service; use a little wine for thy stomach's sake and thine often infirmities. The witnesses note drink no longer water, but use a little wine. The witnesses note for thy stomach's sake and thine often infirmities.

The witnesses note the fatherly counsel for Timothy's health — to use a little wine for his weak stomach; temperance good, but needless austerity not to ruin needed health.

So the witnesses make the verse the kindly care for the minister's health. The lesson the expositors draw is that Timothy is to care for his health, using a little wine as medicine, not ruining it by needless severity. The witnesses note the no longer water: "drink no longer water" — Gill notes Timothy drank only water in abstemious living; the no longer water; the only water; the abstemious; the strict; the his living. The expositors observe the commendable yet: his strictness was commendable, yet his health mattered; the commendable yet; the strictness; the his health; the mattered; the care. The witnesses press the little wine: "but use a little wine" — but use a little wine (moderately, as medicine); the little wine; the moderately; the medicine; the a little; the use. The expositors draw the lesson on the stomach: "for thy stomach's sake" — for his weak stomach; the stomach; the weak; the his stomach; the the ailment; the medicine. The witnesses observe the often infirmities: "and thine often infirmities" — and his frequent infirmities; the often infirmities; the frequent; the weakness; the ailments; the sick. The expositors note the not ruin health: the minister must not by austerity ruin the health he needs; the not ruin health; the austerity; the the health; the needed; the the work. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the drink no longer water but use a little wine, the for thy stomach's sake and thine often infirmities, and the care for health. The verse thus records the kindly care for the minister's health — teaching that Timothy is to care for his health, not ruining it by needless severity.
24:
"Some men's sins are open beforehand, going before to judgment; and some men they follow after." The witnesses note that some sins are manifest, others hidden until later. Gill notes some men are such open and notorious sinners that there is no need of inquiry about them, or witnesses to their character; they prevent and supersede any formal process, and Timothy could have no difficulty with such. The witnesses note Paul observing that some men's sins are open beforehand, going before to judgment, while other men's sins follow after; the discernment that some sins are plain and others come to light later — that Paul, returning (after the personal word of v.23) to the caution about ordaining and judging men, gives a maxim of discernment: "some men's sins are open beforehand" — so manifest that they go before to judgment, plain to all without inquiry — while "some men they follow after," that is, other men's sins are hidden for a time and come to light only later; so that Timothy, in proving men for office, must reckon with both, not being deceived because a man's faults are not yet seen; some men's sins are open beforehand, going before to judgment; and some men they follow after. The witnesses note some men's sins are open beforehand, going before to judgment. The witnesses note and some men they follow after.

The witnesses note that some sins are open and plain, going before to judgment, while others stay hidden and come to light later; Timothy must reckon with both in proving men.

So the witnesses make the verse the discernment of open and hidden sins. The lesson the expositors draw is that some men's sins are manifest at once, others come to light only later, so Timothy must prove men carefully. The witnesses note the open beforehand: "some men's sins are open beforehand" — Gill notes some are open, notorious sinners, needing no inquiry; the open beforehand; the notorious; the manifest; the no inquiry; the plain. The expositors observe the going before: "going before to judgment" — going before to judgment, plain to all; the going before; the to judgment; the plain; the to all; the manifest. The witnesses press the follow after: "and some men they follow after" — other men's sins follow after (hidden, then revealed); the follow after; the hidden; the later; the revealed; the come to light. The expositors draw the lesson on the hidden for a time: some sins are hidden for a time and come to light later; the hidden for a time; the later; the revealed; the come to light; the hidden. The witnesses observe the reckon with both: Timothy must reckon with both in proving men; the reckon with both; the proving men; the for office; the both; the careful. The expositors note the not deceived: not deceived because a man's faults are not yet seen; the not deceived; the not yet seen; the faults; the careful; the proving. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the some men's sins are open beforehand going before to judgment, the and some men they follow after, and the discernment of sins. The verse thus records the discernment of open and hidden sins — teaching that some men's sins are manifest at once, others come to light only later, so Timothy must prove men carefully.
25:
"Likewise also the good works of some are manifest beforehand; and they that are otherwise cannot be hid." The witnesses note that good works too, whether open or hidden, will at last appear. Gill notes the characters of some men are so fair and bright, and it is so clear they have received the grace of God and are sound and godly, that they prevent any formal inquiry; their good works are manifest. The witnesses note Paul observing that the good works of some are manifest beforehand, and those otherwise cannot be hid; the assurance that worth, like sin, will in the end be revealed — that Paul completes the maxim on its brighter side: as with sins, so with "good works" — "the good works of some are manifest beforehand" (plainly seen, marking the fit man at once), and even those that "are otherwise" (not so evident, the quiet goodness that is hidden for a while) "cannot be hid" — they too will in time come to light; so that Timothy may take heart, for genuine worth, though sometimes unseen at first, will surely be made known, as truly as sin will; the good works of some are manifest beforehand; and they that are otherwise cannot be hid. The witnesses note the good works of some are manifest beforehand. The witnesses note they that are otherwise cannot be hid.

The witnesses note that good works, like sins, will at last appear — some manifest at once, others hidden for a while, yet none can finally be hid; genuine worth will be known.

So the witnesses make the verse the certainty that good works will be revealed. The lesson the expositors draw is that genuine worth, like sin, will surely come to light, whether seen at once or hidden for a time. The witnesses note the manifest: "the good works of some are manifest beforehand" — Gill notes some men's good works are plainly seen; the manifest; the good works; the plainly seen; the the fit man; the open. The expositors observe the fair characters: some men's characters are so fair and bright they need no inquiry; the fair characters; the bright; the the grace; the godly; the manifest. The witnesses press the otherwise: "and they that are otherwise" — those good works not so evident; the otherwise; the not evident; the quiet goodness; the hidden; the for a while. The expositors draw the lesson on the cannot be hid: "cannot be hid" — they too cannot finally be hid; the cannot be hid; the come to light; the revealed; the at last; the made known. The witnesses observe the worth revealed: genuine worth will surely be made known; the worth revealed; the genuine; the made known; the revealed; the surely. The expositors note the take heart: Timothy may take heart, for worth will be known as truly as sin; the take heart; the worth; the known; the as sin; the assured. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the good works of some are manifest beforehand, the they that are otherwise cannot be hid, and the certainty of worth revealed. The verse thus records the certainty that good works will be revealed — teaching that genuine worth, like sin, will surely come to light.
